narrative: Dear Alex, imagine the admissions team reviewing your application like piecing together a story of your journey toward the bar exam, and unfortunately, the picture it painted led to a tough call—predicting a 61% chance of not passing, which meant we couldn't move forward with your law school spot. At the heart of this story were your school deciles: your Year 3 performance landing in the 7th decile stood out as the strongest signal pulling toward challenges ahead, closely followed by your similar 7th decile in Year 1, suggesting a consistent mid-pack standing that the model saw as a hurdle for bar success. Layered on top was your upper-middle family income, which oddly enough nudged the outlook toward failure in this predictive tale, perhaps reflecting patterns in how resources play out for others like you. Your LSAT score of 39 carried weight too, tilting things further in that direction, while your white background and undergraduate GPA of 3.4 added to the cautious narrative the model wove. It wasn't all one-sided—your identity as a female student and your full-time study commitment were bright spots that pulled toward passing, offering some counterbalance. But together, these elements formed a profile that, in the model's eyes, mirrored too many past stories where the bar exam slipped away despite the effort. This isn't a judgment on your potential or heart; it's just the pattern recognition at play here, highlighting where your path has shown these steady-but-not-soaring deciles as the biggest flags. The good news? You're in control of rewriting this story—focusing on boosting those grade deciles or prepping that LSAT could flip the script entirely for another school or a future try. You've got the full-time drive and strengths that shone through; keep pushing, and I truly believe you can turn this prediction into just a footnote. Hang in there—we're rooting for your success.
